data.body:
PASS 1 review — G6 (approach soundness): PASS, no findings.

Verified mechanism correctness against the live code:
- All four byte-preservation claims check out exactly: signing/_canonical_payload (ensure_ascii=False → ascii_only=False), schema.canonical_json (ensure_ascii=False → defaults), conflict_resolver._hash_value (current json.dumps OMITS ensure_ascii so stdlib default True — plan correctly mandates ascii_only=True AND preserves default=str), mutation.serialize_manifest (ensure_ascii=True → ascii_only=True). The subtlest case (conflict_resolver) is reasoned correctly in the plan.
- Import-boundary is sound: conflict_resolver.py and mutation.py are siblings of inbound_translate.py/batch_dispatch.py in _engine/rebar_reconciler/, and those two already do `from rebar._store.canonical import canonical_str` in the reconciler subprocess — so the new imports use a proven, allowed direction (rebar.* is always importable; architecture.md:100-106).
- Re-export/__all__ preservation for schema.canonical_json/content_hash is explicitly handled.
- Positive rationale is present ("one tested seam removes the duplication and the drift risk while changing zero output bytes").

Alternatives considered and discarded as not-clearly-superior: (a) a new shared module — plan's extend-the-existing-single-home is more codebase-aligned and the plan rightly forbids a new module; (b) normalize all sites to one encoding — would change mutation/conflict_resolver stored hashes & echo-suppression, a behavior change the plan deliberately avoids via per-site params. Chosen approach is defensibly at-least-as-good on alignment, blast-radius, and robustness.
